title: False Teachers and Their Destruction
subtitle: Destructive heresies, examples of judgment, and the corruption of the false prophets

heb.paragraph: Destructive heresies - the term denotes divisive teaching that splits the community. The content is denial of the Master who bought them.
Many will follow their sensuality - false teaching leads to immoral living. Doctrine and ethics are inseparable.

heb.gloss: to cast into Tartarus, consign to hell
heb.paragraph: God cast angels into hell (Tartarus) - a term from Greek mythology adapted for divine judgment. Even angels are not exempt.

panels.ctx: False prophets arose among the people, and false teachers will arise among you. They will secretly bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them. Many will follow their sensuality, and the way of truth will be blasphemed. In their greed they will exploit you with false words. Their condemnation is not idle; their destruction is not asleep. God did not spare angels when they sinned but cast them into hell. He did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah. He condemned Sodom and Gomorrah to extinction but rescued righteous Lot. The Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials and to keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment - especially those who indulge in defiling passion and despise authority.

cross.note: Parallel passage on false teachers and examples of judgment.
The sons of God and daughters of men - angelic sin.
Beware of false prophets who come in sheep clothing.

notes.note: False teachers will bring destructive heresies, denying the Master. Many will follow their sensuality. Their condemnation is coming.
God did not spare sinning angels, or the ancient world, or Sodom - but he rescued Noah and Lot.
The Lord knows how to rescue the godly and keep the unrighteous for judgment - especially those who despise authority.
Calvin notes that denying the Master is not necessarily explicit atheism but practical denial through immoral living.
The three examples (angels, flood, Sodom) establish a pattern: God judges the wicked and rescues the righteous. History teaches.
The term hairesis originally meant choice or school of thought but came to mean divisive faction - the source of English heresy.
Tartarus appears only here in the NT. In Greek mythology it was the deepest abyss, below Hades. Peter uses it for the prison of fallen angels.
Schreiner emphasizes the correlation between false teaching and immorality. Bad theology produces bad behavior; the false teachers prove this.
Davids notes the parallels with Jude and suggests both draw on common tradition about false teachers. The examples are standard Jewish polemic.

heb.paragraph: Bold and willful - the false teachers are arrogant risk-takers who blaspheme what they do not understand.
Blots and blemishes - the false teachers defile the community. They are moral stains on the fellowship.
Accursed children - literally children of curse. They are destined for judgment; curse defines their identity.

panels.ctx: Bold and willful, they do not tremble as they blaspheme the glorious ones. Even angels, greater in power, do not pronounce a blasphemous judgment against them before the Lord. But these, like irrational animals, creatures of instinct born to be caught and destroyed - they will be destroyed in their destruction, suffering wrong as the wage for their wrongdoing. They count it pleasure to revel in the daytime. Blots and blemishes, they revel while feasting with you. They have eyes full of adultery that never cease from sin, enticing unstable souls. They have hearts trained in greed. Accursed children! Forsaking the right way, they have followed Balaam who loved gain from wrongdoing but was rebuked by a donkey.

cross.note: Parallel description of false teachers blaspheming, following Balaam.
Balaam counsel led Israel to sin at Peor.
The teaching of Balaam - eating idol food, sexual immorality.

notes.note: Bold and willful, they blaspheme the glorious ones. Like irrational animals, they will be destroyed in their destruction.
They revel in daytime. Blots and blemishes feasting with you. Eyes full of adultery, enticing unstable souls. Hearts trained in greed.
They have forsaken the right way and followed Balaam who loved wrongful gain. A donkey rebuked the prophet madness.
Calvin sees the animal comparison as describing those ruled by appetite rather than reason. They follow instinct to destruction.
Balaam is the prototype of ministerial greed - using spiritual office for financial gain. The donkey rebuke is divine irony.
The glorious ones are probably angelic beings. False teachers speak evil of what they do not understand.
Reveling in daytime was considered especially shameful - decent people caroused at night; daytime revelry was brazen.
Schreiner emphasizes the vivid, almost excessive rhetoric. Peter piles up images to create moral revulsion against the false teachers.
Davids notes that Balaam became a stock figure for greedy false prophets in Jewish tradition. The name alone evokes the type.

heb.paragraph: Waterless springs - they promise refreshment but deliver nothing. The image is devastating in an arid climate.
Mists driven by a storm - they are insubstantial and uncontrollable. They follow the wind of their passions.
Slaves of corruption - they promise freedom but are themselves enslaved. The enslaved cannot liberate.

panels.ctx: These are waterless springs and mists driven by a storm. For them the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved. Speaking loud boasts of folly, they entice by sensual passions of the flesh those who are barely escaping from error. They promise freedom but are themselves slaves of corruption - whatever overcomes a person, to that he is enslaved. For if, after escaping the defilements of the world through knowledge of the Lord Jesus Christ, they are again entangled and overcome, the last state is worse than the first. It would have been better never to have known the way of righteousness than after knowing it to turn back. The true proverb: The dog returns to its own vomit, and the sow, after washing, to wallow in the mire.

cross.note: Parallel images: waterless clouds, wandering stars, gloom of darkness.
Everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin.
The last state of that person is worse than the first.
Like a dog that returns to his vomit is a fool who repeats his folly.

notes.note: Waterless springs, mists driven by storm. Gloom reserved for them. They entice the unstable by sensuality, promising freedom while being slaves.
If after escaping defilements through knowledge of Christ they are again entangled, the last state is worse. Dog returns to vomit; sow to mire.
Calvin sees the promised freedom as antinomian license. They claim freedom from law but are slaves to lust.
The warning is severe: apostasy is worse than never believing. Knowledge increases responsibility; rejection after knowledge is grievous.
Waterless springs would be particularly disappointing in the ancient Near East where water was precious and springs were sought eagerly.
The dog proverb is from Proverbs 26:11. The sow proverb is not in Scripture but was proverbial. Both animals were considered unclean.
Schreiner emphasizes that vv. 20-22 describe apostasy. The false teachers once knew the truth but turned away. Their end is worse than their beginning.
Davids notes the pastoral concern: the false teachers are not just wrong but dangerous. They entice vulnerable believers barely escaping error.

rows.label: vv. 1-10a
rows.text: False teachers coming; examples of judgment

rows.label: vv. 17-22
rows.text: Waterless springs; worse than the first

lit.note: Chapter 2 is an extended polemic against false teachers. Peter uses OT examples, vivid imagery, and sharp rhetoric to warn against their influence.

themes.note: Judgment dominates the chapter through the OT examples and warnings. Justice is strong in the retribution theme. Holiness appears in the contrast between the righteous and the wicked.
